article, 156 pages long, is devoted to the development of the theory of algebraic functions, as it is applicable in the case of universal Cremona transformations and Cremona groups. The subject is presented in such a way as to make the article suitable as an introduction to the general theory of discontinuous groups. The second volume, not yet published, will take up the applications to Riemann's surfaces, differential equations, algebraic differential equations, and automorphic functions, and will contain a special theory of Cremona transformations. Along with the theory of automorphic functions will be developed the analytic theory in close connection with the abstract algebraic conception of groups.

G. A. Miller.

A Memoir on the Integration of Analytic Functions.* By W. F. OsGOOD.

By the integration of an analytic function Riemann understood, not merely the determination of a single-valued function whose complete derivative shall be equal to a given function, but the determination of the manifoldness of functions that can arise in this way on the Riemann surface which belongs to the given function. The determination of this manifoldness of functions depends on the study of the integrals taken round closed paths. In the present memoir the treatment of this problem which Riemann developed for abelian integrals in his memoir of 1857 is extended to more general cases. The theorem that every local analytic function can be continued analytically along any path not passing through a singularity of the differential equation is shown to depend on a general theorem in implicit functions. It is natural, when considering this question of analytic continuation, to study it from the point of view of differential equations. This is the standpoint taken in the present memoir and the results yielded in this way are both general in form and capable of immediate application to particular problems like the inversion of the hyperelliptic integrals.

W. F. OsGOoD.
